questions.question:
Consider the two program segments below:
a.  for i := 1 to f(x) by 1 do
      S
    end
b.  i := 1;
    while i <= f(x) do
      S
      i := i + 1
    end

Under what conditions are these two programs equivalent? Treat S as any sequence of statements and f as a function.

A. They are always equivalent.
B. They are equivalent only if S does not modify x.
C. They are equivalent only if f(x) >= 1.
D. They are equivalent only if S does not modify x and f(x) >= 1.
questions.answer: B. They are equivalent only if S does not modify x.
questions.to_find: The condition under which the for-loop and while-loop segments are equivalent.

understand.plain: We have a for-loop that calls f(x) once (to set the upper bound) and a while-loop that calls f(x) on every iteration to re-check the condition. If S changes x, then f(x) can produce a different value each time in the while-loop, making the two loops behave differently. The question asks when they behave the same.

keywords.explain: In many languages, the upper bound of a for-loop is evaluated only once at the start. Here 'for i := 1 to f(x)' computes f(x) once.
keywords.example: If f(x)=5 initially, the for-loop runs exactly 5 times regardless of what S does to x.

keywords.explain: A while-loop re-evaluates its condition before every iteration. If the condition calls f(x), then f(x) is computed fresh each time.
keywords.example: If S changes x so that f(x) grows, the while-loop may run more iterations than the equivalent for-loop.

understand.visual_alt: For-loop evaluates f(x) once; while-loop evaluates f(x) on every iteration check. If S modifies x, the two loops can diverge.

given.aim: Determine when 'for i:=1 to f(x) by 1 do S end' is equivalent to 'i:=1; while i<=f(x) do S; i:=i+1 end'.

terms.meaning: A function applied to x that produces the loop bound.
terms.connects: In the for-loop, f(x) is evaluated once before the first iteration. In the while-loop it is re-evaluated each time.

terms.meaning: An arbitrary sequence of statements forming the loop body.
terms.example: S could be 'x := x + 1' which changes x.
terms.connects: If S modifies x, the while-loop's f(x) changes each iteration, causing divergence from the for-loop.

terms.meaning: Two loops are equivalent if they execute the body the same number of times and produce identical results.
terms.example: Both loops running exactly n times with the same body produce the same outcome.
terms.connects: Equivalence breaks if S modifies x because f(x) gets re-evaluated differently in the while-loop.

given.plan: Compare how f(x) is evaluated in each loop. The for-loop evaluates f(x) once; the while-loop evaluates f(x) each iteration. They differ exactly when x changes inside S (changing f(x) mid-loop). If S does not modify x, f(x) stays constant, so both loops run the same number of times.

steps.title: Analyze for-loop behavior
steps.apply: In 'for i := 1 to f(x) by 1 do S end', f(x) is evaluated once before the loop begins. Let n = f(x) at the start. The loop runs for i = 1, 2, ..., n regardless of what S does.
steps.note: The for-loop's iteration count is fixed at the moment of entry.

steps.title: Analyze while-loop behavior
steps.apply: In 'while i <= f(x) do S; i:=i+1 end', the condition i <= f(x) is tested before each iteration. If S changes x, then f(x) can differ from one test to the next. This means the loop may run a different number of times than the for-loop.
steps.note: The while-loop re-evaluates f(x) every iteration, unlike the for-loop.

steps.title: Determine equivalence condition
steps.apply: If S does NOT modify x, then f(x) remains the same value throughout execution of the while-loop. In that case the while-loop condition 'i <= f(x)' behaves as 'i <= n' (constant), and the while-loop also runs exactly n times. The two loops produce identical results. If S can modify x, they may diverge.
steps.note: The condition is that S does not modify x; there is no additional constraint on the sign of f(x) because both loops handle f(x) = 0 or negative values consistently (zero iterations) when S doesn't modify x.

solution.result: The two programs are equivalent only if S does not modify x (Option B).

questions.formula_note: Semantics of for-loop vs while-loop: for-loop evaluates upper bound once; while-loop re-evaluates condition every iteration. Equivalence requires the condition expression to be stable across iterations.

questions.question:
Consider the following PASCAL program segment:

if i mod 2 = 0 then
  while i >= 0 do
  begin
    i := i div 2;
    if i mod 2 <> 0 then
      i := i - 1
    else
      i := i * 2
  end;

An appropriate loop-invariant for the while-loop is ________
questions.to_find: The loop invariant for the while-loop in the given PASCAL segment.

understand.plain: A loop invariant is a condition that is true before the loop starts and remains true after every iteration. The loop is entered only when i is even (i mod 2 = 0). Inside, we first halve i; if the result is odd we subtract 1 to make it even again, and if even we double it. We need to identify what property of i is preserved throughout.

keywords.explain: A predicate (condition) that holds before the loop, after each iteration, and when the loop exits. Used to reason about correctness.
keywords.example: For a loop computing sum 1..n, the invariant might be 'sum = 1+2+...+i' at the start of iteration i.

keywords.explain: A number is even if its remainder when divided by 2 is zero.

understand.visual_alt: The loop is entered with even i. Each iteration: i = i div 2; if that result is odd, subtract 1 to restore evenness; if even, double it (stays even). So i is always even at the loop guard check.

given.aim: Identify a predicate on i that is true before the while-loop and is preserved by every iteration.

terms.meaning: The if-guard 'i mod 2 = 0' ensures i is even when the while-loop is first reached.
terms.example: If i = 6, loop is entered; if i = 5, loop is skipped.
terms.connects: This gives us the initial invariant candidate: i is even.

terms.meaning: Integer division of i by 2; result may be odd or even.
terms.connects: After this step i might become odd, but the next if-else restores parity.

terms.meaning: If i becomes odd after halving, we do i := i - 1 to make it even. If already even, we do i := i * 2 (which keeps it even).
terms.connects: The loop body always leaves i even at its end, preserving the invariant.

given.plan: Check that 'i mod 2 = 0' holds: (1) before the loop (guaranteed by the if-guard), (2) at the end of every iteration (shown by case analysis on the result of i div 2), (3) at loop exit (holds trivially). This makes it a valid loop invariant.

steps.title: Verify invariant holds initially
steps.apply: The while-loop is inside 'if i mod 2 = 0 then ...'. So when the while-loop is first reached, i is even. The invariant 'i mod 2 = 0' holds before the first iteration.
steps.note: The outer if-guard is the proof of initialization.

steps.title: Verify invariant is preserved by each iteration
steps.apply:
Assume i is even at the start of an iteration.
- Step: i := i div 2. Result is some integer j (may be odd or even).
  Case 1: j is odd (j mod 2 != 0) -> i := i - 1 = j - 1. Since j is odd, j-1 is even. So i becomes even.
  Case 2: j is even (j mod 2 = 0) -> i := i * 2 = 2j. 2j is always even. So i becomes even.
In both cases i is even at the end of the iteration. The invariant is maintained.
steps.note: The two branches of the inner if-else together guarantee i stays even after every full iteration.

steps.title: Confirm the invariant at loop exit
steps.apply: The loop exits when i < 0 (since the condition is i >= 0). At that point i is still even (the invariant held at the end of the last iteration before the exit check). The invariant 'i mod 2 = 0' holds on exit as well.
steps.note: A loop invariant must hold on exit; here i is negative and even (e.g., -2, -4, ...).

solution.result: The loop invariant is: i mod 2 = 0 (i is always even).

questions.formula_note: Loop invariant identification: find a predicate true at loop entry (from the outer guard), preserved by every iteration (case analysis on loop body), and meaningful at loop exit. Here parity of i is the invariant.

questions.answer: A. n = d_(i+1) d_(i+2) ... d_m  and  rev = d_i d_(i-1) ... d_1
questions.to_find: The loop invariant condition at the end of the i-th iteration of the digit-reversal loop.

understand.plain: The program reverses a number's digits. At each step it peels off the last digit of n (n%10) and appends it to rev (by shifting rev left one decimal place and adding the digit). After i iterations, the last i digits of n have been moved to rev in reverse order, and n has been reduced to its first (m-i) digits.

keywords.explain: n % 10 gives the rightmost (least significant) digit of n.
Shifts the current reversed number one decimal place left and appends the new digit to the right.
keywords.example: rev=12, digit=3 -> rev=123. This builds the reversed number digit by digit.

understand.visual_svg: <svg width="560" height="200" viewBox="0 0 560 200" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x="10" y="10" width="540" height="50" rx="6" class="bg"/><text x="20" y="40" class="ink lbl">n = d1 d2 d3 ... dm  (initial)</text><rect x="10" y="80" width="540" height="50" rx="6" class="hi"/><text x="20" y="110" class="ink lbl">After i iterations: n = d_(i+1)...d_m  |  rev = d_i...d_1</text><rect x="10" y="148" width="540" height="42" rx="6" class="bg"/><text x="20" y="172" class="ink sub">Each iteration peels rightmost digit of n, prepends it to rev (right side)</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: After i iterations: n holds remaining digits d_(i+1) through d_m (the leading portion), rev holds digits d_i down to d_1 (the extracted digits in reverse order, i.e. d_i is most significant of rev and d_1 is least significant).

given.aim: Identify what n and rev hold after exactly i iterations of the while-loop.

terms.meaning: Initial integer with m digits; d_1 is the most significant digit, d_m is the least significant.
terms.connects: Each iteration removes d_(m), d_(m-1),... but actually the digits are peeled from the right: first d_m, then d_(m-1), etc.

terms.meaning: After i iterations, rev contains the last i digits of the original n in reversed order (rightmost digit first).
terms.example: n=1234, after 2 iterations: rev=43 (d_4=4 first, then d_3=3).
terms.connects: This matches option A: rev = d_i d_(i-1) ... d_1, where d_i is the i-th digit from the right.

terms.meaning: After i iterations, n has been integer-divided by 10 i times, so it holds only the first (m-i) digits: d_1 d_2 ... d_(m-i).
terms.connects: In option A notation (where d_1...d_m goes left to right and digits are peeled from the right), n = d_1...d_(m-i) = d_(i+1)...d_m only if indexing is from the right — this matches option A.

given.plan: Trace the loop manually for a small example and match against the four options. Use n=1234 (m=4). After iteration 1: n=123, rev=4. After iteration 2: n=12, rev=43. Check each option.

steps.title: Trace iterations with n=1234
steps.note: The digits are peeled from the right: d_4=4, d_3=3, d_2=2, d_1=1 in that order.

steps.title: Match the trace against the options
steps.apply:
After i=2: n=12 (= d_1 d_2 in left-to-right notation), rev=43 (= d_4 d_3 in left-to-right notation, i.e. d_m d_(m-1)...d_(m-i+1) = d_4 d_3).

Option A says: n = d_(i+1) d_(i+2) ... d_m and rev = d_i d_(i-1) ... d_1.
With digits indexed from LEFT (d_1=1,d_2=2,d_3=3,d_4=4) and i=2:
  n should be d_3 d_4 = 34 -> but n=12, NOT matching if digits indexed from left.

Re-index from right (d_1=4,d_2=3,d_3=2,d_4=1, i.e. d_k = the k-th digit from the RIGHT):
  After i=2: n=12, rev=43.
  Option A: n = d_(i+1)...d_m = d_3 d_4 = 2,1 = 12. Correct!
  Option A: rev = d_i...d_1 = d_2 d_1 = 3,4 = 43 (as a number). Correct!

Option D: n = d_1...d_(m-i) = d_1 d_2 = 4,3 = 43 != 12. Incorrect.
steps.note: The key insight is that the question uses d_1...d_m with d_1 as the RIGHTMOST digit (least significant). Under this convention Option A matches perfectly.

steps.title: Confirm Option A is the loop invariant
steps.apply:
At end of i-th iteration:
- n has been divided by 10 i times, so it retains the digits that were originally in positions i+1 through m (from the right), i.e. n = d_(i+1) d_(i+2) ... d_m.
- rev has accumulated i digits in order d_i, d_(i-1), ..., d_1 (rightmost digits of original n, appended one by one).
This is exactly what Option A states.
steps.note: This is a standard loop invariant for the digit-reversal algorithm.

solution.result: Option A: n = d_(i+1) d_(i+2) ... d_m  and  rev = d_i d_(i-1) ... d_1.

questions.formula_note: Loop invariant for digit reversal: after i iterations, n retains the upper (m-i) digits and rev contains the lower i digits in reversed order, built by the recurrence rev = rev*10 + n%10.
